Extract and structure the following information as valid JSON only (no markdown, no extra text).Return ONLY this JSON structure:{"summary": "2-3 sentence summary of the video content","keyPoints": ["point 1", "point 2", ...],"aiConcepts": ["AI concept 1", "AI concept 2", ...],"technicalDetails": ["tool/framework 1", "implementation detail", ...],"visualContextFlags": [{"timestamp": 120, "reason": "Code demo", "context": "Shows Python implementation"}]}If the video is not about AI/tech, set aiConcepts and technicalDetails to empty arrays but still extract keyPoints.TRANSCRIPT:thank you everyone very much for coming to this course really exciting for me to be here as well in Barcelona first time uh I think a lot of PhD students and maybe other kinds of researchers here as well hands up if you're a PhD student yeah almost everyone hands up if you're not a PhD student okay are you a Masters or undergraduate students or postdocs what are you masses okay masses you're working okay you're just interested okay yeah yeah welcome okay great and I think we've got people online as well right how many people do we have online at the moment okay and they can all he and see me okay it's all good all right great so just want to say if you feel you have trouble understanding me just put your hand up and like ask me to repeat it's no problem or ask me to talk slowly no problem at all okay so my name is Stefano Al I'm associate professor at the University of Edinburgh in the school of informatics and I'm very happy to be here to teach you a little bit about multi-agent enforcement learning which is a really hot topic in Ai and machine learning and something that we have worked on a lot so I'm first going to spend just a few minutes to introduce the course to give you some background information and then we're going to switch gears and get into the slides for the course okay so um let me first introduce my group we're the autonomous agents research group and the schol informatics here you see a subset of the people we're about 20 people postto and PhD students and um we're very much hardcore focused on deep reinforcement learning multi-age reinforcement learning and more broadly speaking machine learning algorithms for autonomous systems control and decision making that's what we do basically that's our daily business we are hosted in the school of informatics and I want to say um School of informatics is by a wide margin the largest computer science department in the entire country it has about 160 full-time professors it's very large has a long history as well a lot of research going on it's also historically one of the oldest AI institutes in the world so in 1963 there were only three AI labs in the world at MIT at Stanford and at Edinburgh a lot of the early work came came from from Edinburgh and um it's also ranked by the UK government as the number one computer science department in the UK for research power and impact uh so it's it's a very strong place and if any of you ever visit Edinburgh it's beautiful city but feel free to reach out then we'll be happy to show you around and team me up with some people in my group and show you our department there's a website here which has a lot of resources for PhD students and other researchers I'll say more about that in in in a second okay so broadly speaking we want to create autonomous agents that can accomplish tasks in complex D dynamic environments I'm showing you a few example applications that we actually actively working on on the left there's an example where we have warehousing application I'll show you some videos of this later in the center there is an example of self-driving I was for a number of years involved with a company where we were building self-driving vehicles and our company was bought by Bosch a couple of years ago and now we think with Bosch we Europe's largest level four self driving team and then there's a few other example applications so in all the these applications what they have in common is that you have to develop these highly sophisticated autonomous systems that can process constant streams of data and make safe and informed decisions and something else that's in common here and that's kind of the focus of what we're doing today is that there's not just a single actor there are multiple actors that coexist and and they have to interact in some useful meaningful way to solve a task so it's a multi-agent system uh I mentioned the website earlier the website has a lot of useful resources and I want to just take a minute to tell you about that first we have a Blog which you can find here uh there's a number of blog posts on there which uh summarize all kinds of useful information many of these blog posts are summaries or tutorial style posts which introduce Concepts inforcement learning and multi systems um so for example in deep reinforcement learning there's a lot of secret Source a lot of secret implementation uh sort of tricks that you have to use to make it work unfortunately these things are never explained in the papers but when you look at the Cod it's full of stuff and we decided to collect it all in a blog post you can you can see it uh other blog posts about um an online talk series that we organized at the alen touring Institute um multi-agent environments basic mistakes that were made done in sort of propagated in the research community in the early days of multii Med post learning uh so lots of really useful blog posts for you to check out the other useful resource I want to share with you is our um code repository on giup we have uh many actively maintained code repositories with all kinds of algorithm implementations that you can use but maybe the most important one especially for today is our epar code base this is a research prototype code base for multi-agent reinforcement learning it has a standardized interface it has I think 10 mature implementations of algorithms it has all kinds of environments you can plug and play and it has all kinds of configuration um sort of knobs so if you want to quickly get into multi enforcement learning and plug-and playay this is a starting point for you we published this at nurs a couple years ago and it's been picked up by the community a lot so lots of other papers are building on it for example or building on the algorithms so it's it's a good starting point okay but maybe the most important resource I want to share with you is this new book uh that we just finished writing and published with MIT press this is the first and only textbook ever that introduces this field of multi-agent reinforcement learning it's uh published with MIT press it's uh available on the website here available for free in fact you can get the book PDF you can also get all the slides that I'm going to be using in this week and also there's another code base that we released with the book which is designed to be very easy to use so in contrast to EPL the thing I said earlier which is very sophisticated this code base is very easy like small self-contained easy to read that was the point of that code base so if you're really very new to this type of work then this might be the best in point for the book is uh designed to be self-contained it has chapters on uh reinforcement learning game theory deep learning deep single agent reinforcement learning and then talks a lot about multi-agent reinforcement learning and Engineering aspects and you can already pre-order the book on all kinds of shops like Amazon and it will come out in print just a few days before Christmas so everyone please go ahead and pre-order the book and help me live a more luxurious life okay uh yeah by the way a question so we're really happy with the um the design for the book cover does anyone have an idea what this might mean this actually has a pretty cool meaning the the spiral you see there don't be shy any hands up if you have any ideas about what that might mean yeah okay interesting but no about interesting idea yes any other ideas anyone online who wants to guess okay I'll tell you so this is an abstract um graphical depiction of a mathematical process where two agents are repeatedly interacting and in the process they are learning to converge to a fix point which is in the center uh and in this case it would be a ntion equilibrium so imagine the two agents play a game of rock paper scissors the two dots are the two agents the two dots are of the two agents the links between the two dots means one interaction okay one one time playing and they keep repeating the interaction and and then they spiral into the center and in the center there's this fix Point solution which is a as cilium which in Rick rock paper scissors means that everyone plays all actions uniformly randomly it's pretty cool I think it's it's beautifully sort of summarized uh abstract depiction anyway this is the book and I if you have the time I recommend that you check out the chapters before the days this week so you can have an easier following the lectures or you can go back to the chapters after the lectures and just to revisit course overview so we have a few days here uh we'll be mostly be following the chapters in the book uh for which you can get the slides on the book website some other things we're going to spend more time on some other things we're going to be doing fairly quickly and some things we're going to be skipping all together okay so um this will be a sort of a dynamically paced course sometimes we spent more time sometimes we be quite fast-paced but you can always go back to the book chapter and take your time there will also be coding exercises so one of my uh co-authors just for this course he put together three very small coding exercises that shouldn't take more than maybe one two or three hours that you can do yourself in the afternoon and the link to that will be released on the slack I think everyone here should be on the slack and there's a channel for coding exercises so this is optional we're not going to be discussing this much we we won't be grading anything but it comes with files that explain step by step how to run some of these algorithms how to get some results and get you can get started and and get playing so it's completely up to you whether you want to do this all right so just a warm up um I want to ask just Ron so why do you want to learn about Mar m enforcement learning and what do you want to do with it ends up I really would like to know from you why you actually care about this don't be shy right it's just interesting we had to start a conversation I think we have a microphone there yes so I'm working on uh football analytics and okay as an application for multiple agents on the soccer field that's why and there are some in advances right now that use reinforcement learning and imitation learning right now but I think multi- agent is a more applicable scenario here how how can you use multi-age reinforcement learning for football analytics so simulating uh the game in general and getting more results so we don't have a lot of counterfactual information right now from the data that we have okay so creating that so you want to learn policies for your players to see what they would do in certain situations okay interesting yeah right any anyone else why do you care about Marl what do you want to do ends up I so uh I'm I'm working on automated negotiation and um I think it could be one way to to train agents to uh to learn how to negotiate with each other mhm or vice versa I think negotiation could actually maybe help existing moral uh uh problems or Solutions okay to to improve if the agents can negotiate with each other how to solve a problem maybe in that way it could help okay and I've recently seen a lot of papers uh talking about moral in combination with I want to say this now rather than at the end just make sure I don't forget after this if you want to get even deeper into the field there's a few more events coming up that I'm helping to organize in the UK there is the alent touring Institute which is the national Ai and data Science Institute uh named after alent Turing and we are organizing the second UK malti Asian system Symposium which brings together all kinds of labs in the UK and Ireland that do malti Asian system research and you'd be welcome to come if if you uh if you happen to be in London at the time and and we actually so there will be no attendance fee so you can come if you're a student and then the second thing is I'm program chair for a big reinforcement learning conference which will be in Dublin next year in in June and and so lots of big names in reinforcement learning and multii reinforcement learning and Robotics and so on will be there but also there will be lots of people who care about cognitive science psychology and behavioral economics who also care about reinforcement learning so it brings these communities together and there's a website here rm.org that you can check out already good I think with that I'm going to switch to the actual uh book slides last see okay so introduction uh as I said you can get all the slides online from the book website uh in fact we're going to try and polish them even more but the current versions you can get already on the book website uh by the way if you know any professors who care about reinforcement learning or multi reinforcement learning and would like to teach please tell them that there's this amazing book now and also the slides and they can use the slides and the book for free to to teach so we we want want to spread it as much as possible okay so I mentioned ready the book you can get on the website uh introduction so we're going to talk just a little bit about multi-agent systems what are they why do we care and then what is learning in multi-agent systems and some challenges involved as well okay introduction if there's any questions uh feel free to just put your hands up okay we can uh get your question all right so multi reinforcement learning or Marl is about algorithms that try and find Optimal decision policy for two or more artificial in agents that interact and coexist in a shared environment okay so the algorithms want to learn policies for multiple agents that coexist in a shared environment we use reinforcement learning it's in the name so we want to interact and Learn by obser observing what's coming what's happening if we take certain actions uh and we want to improve over time the goal is to learn these optimal policies there's lots of uh exciting applications you can try and uh tackle with multi reinforcement learning is just four examples there is a computer games I'm going to show you a video in just a second autonomous driving mentioned earlier something I was heavily involved in where we can use this uh robot Factor where show we show in a video in a second as well automated trading for example eBay you can have automated agents or on um uh travel agency websites you could have an automated agent that you know organize your whole trip let me switch uh slides again cuz I've got a few videos so who knows what this is can anyone see what kind of game this is yes Starcraft 2 yes and uh this is just a sort of an example I wanted to show you for something that we did so I'm going to show you what's happening here there's two teams uh this is a real computer game Starcraft 2 it's a super popular computer game there's professional Leagues with people who make money living playing this game basically and and it's huge huge fan base we control the red team which has a number of different units there's these marines that can shoot and then there's these bigger units that have bigger guns basically and then there's these flying units that can heal then there's the blue team which we're trying to destroy and that's controlled by a built-in AI basically and it looks like this so we use multi reinforcement learning on the red team has first spread out to increase fire patterns and you can see that the healing unit is focusing on the high high energy assets to keep them alive for as long as possible you can see that our units have learned to concentrate fire on the center points and then they take out the high energy assets of the opponent last and basically we learned to completely dominate the opponent very quickly we did not tell our team anything about teamwork or strategies we didn't tell it to spread out we didn't tell it to focus fire patterns we didn't tell it to heal in a particular way we left it to the algorithm and the algorithm learned on its own how to develop these sophisticated teamwork strategies it's a pretty cool thing if you think about right it's this complicated game and the algorithm figures out a good way to do teamwork so that's uh like a very basic example of um a small task and multi-i re enforcement learning but actually you can scale this up and do some pretty big things so who knows about Alpha star who who has heard about alpast star hands up okay some people Alpha star was one of these big sh of projects from Deep Mind where they basically played the entire game of Starcraft 2 not just a sort of a isolated battle scene but actually they played the whole thing from start to finish against professional players so that means you start with just a building and some building units you have to build your whole base you have to collect resources you have to build your whole Army you have to work in partial observability when there's fog of War you cannot see everything you have to spread out collect information you have to then harass and attack your opponent and do some pretty sophisticated uh um attack Maneuvers and strategies and basically here's a video you can find many videos on YouTube where they look at the replay and then these uh moderators basically they look at what's happening and then basically in this case there's a there's a guy on YouTube who is basically talking you through what's happening I'm going to mute him just about my voice now but basically uh there is um in this case Alpha star controls the uh one of those races which is called um uh uh protos and then there's the buil an AI which controls the Zer which are these alien guys and basically you know you start from zero you you build your building you collect resources and you play all this entire game basically it's it's it's really quite impressive if you see what kinds of Maneuvers uh the protos player the built-in the um the multi reinforcement learning approach discovers basically the way it's trained is with a leak or populationbased training it's called so you train a policy using deep learning for your own team and then basically you repeatedly expose your own policy your own team against a pool of different kinds of opponents that you collect over time basically that's what's called population Based training and in that sense you can grow and become better and better and better you can identify weaknesses that you then exploit you also identify your own weaknesses that you then have to close and you you want to also remain resilient against these previous attacks that you saw so you have to keep them in the pool as a type of memory basically and you can train and train become really strong okay so these are computer games but what can you do in real here's an example for a warehousing application that we are actively building in my lab with a very large Warehouse automation company called dematic so dematic operate everywhere there this gigantic Warehouse automation company they build these very high density robot um systems for um uh warehousing systems and basically we want to use multi-age reinforcement learning to see if we can optimize the operations of these systems systems and here is actually a few snapshots for a high fidelity simulator that we built with their engineers and these are real warehouses uh different places in in Europe that we represent so there's a small medium-sized one large warehouse and a disjoint one the disjoint one means that there's a sort of a cold compartment and a warm compartment and they need to be kept separate because of the uh the thermodynamics and basically what's happening is that there's a bunch of humans we call them Pickers because their task is to pick items from the chefs they Pickers um and then there's the AGS which is a bunch of robots that coexist in the warehouse with the humans and the robots tasks is to be at the right place at the right time to collect those items from the humans and bring them back to the packaging station and we want to use Mage reinforcement running to basically optimize the hacker of the system so we train policies for for both the robots and the humans uh the policies are displayed to the humans on an iPad basically and for the robots they they go into the brain and then they operate in this Warehouse so here's example for a real Warehouse in Germany for a car parts manufacturer and basically initially you see that the agents are swarming out and then the orders keep coming in order order order imagine like an Amazon warehouse with lots of requests and orders for different items and basically the the different agents they walk work through the um Warehouse to basically work through all the different orders that keep coming in and this looks like a beehive to you it's like no discern logic but he can do a lot of analysis and understand that they're doing some really sophisticated ways to coordinate basically with individual robots Sur servicing multiple humans at the same time and as a result of this we actually recently opened a real prototype test facility in Germany in a city called damad uh sorry dund where we actually building these uh real systems and we transferring the train policies into the real uh environment now and the dematic will actually commercialize this as a product as well so this I think we can say this will be the world's first real industry scale application of multi-agent reinforcement learning it's a really nice result okay so this was just to show you a couple of videos for motivation and I'm going to try and go back to the other slide okay back here so just to get you excited about some cool things you can do all right so what's a multi-agent system I think we need to talk about this just to make sure we all on the same page multi system primarily is defined by two things first there's the environment environment is a physical uh environment for example a warehouse or a software defined environment like eBay for example or a computer game uh and basically there is a state that evolves over time which is influenced by the actions of the agents that live in this environment so that gets us to the second point the the second part of the environment is the uh or the the multi-agent system is these agents okay agents are virtual entities pieces of software basically that receive information about the state of the environment enironment they can see certain things using different modalities and then they can CH certain actions to influence the state of the environment for example they can walk around they can pick up things they can sort of shoot in Starcraft and that sort of thing and importantly these agents are goal directed so they want to maximize what's called their Rewards or the returns we're going to talk about it in the course of this week but basically they they're goal oriented which means they have defined goals and they working they they're choosing

editmore_vert{"summary": "Professor Stefano Albrecht introduces Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL), detailing its theoretical foundations and practical applications in robotics and gaming. He highlights resources from the University of Edinburgh, including the EPyMARL codebase and the first comprehensive MARL textbook published by MIT Press.","keyPoints": ["MARL focuses on training multiple autonomous agents to interact and solve tasks in shared dynamic environments.","The University of Edinburgh's School of Informatics is a historic leader in AI research, hosting one of the first three AI labs in the world.","Practical applications for MARL include warehouse automation, Level 4 self-driving vehicles, and automated trading.","EPyMARL is a research-grade codebase designed to standardize multi-agent reinforcement learning implementations.","The MIT Press MARL textbook is presented as a self-contained resource covering Game Theory, Deep Learning, and RL."],"aiConcepts": ["Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL)","Deep Reinforcement Learning","Nash Equilibrium","Game Theory","Population-Based Training","Partial Observability","Counterfactual Information","Imitation Learning"],"technicalDetails": ["EPyMARL codebase (GitHub)","Starcraft 2 (SMAC environment)","Alpha Star (DeepMind project)","High-fidelity warehouse simulators","Level 4 autonomous driving systems","Python implementation 'secret sauce' tricks"],"visualContextFlags": [{"timestamp": 630, "reason": "Game demo", "context": "Starcraft 2 gameplay showing MARL-controlled units coordinating fire and healing strategies."},{"timestamp": 720, "reason": "Project overview", "context": "DeepMind's Alpha Star project showcasing full-game Starcraft 2 strategic maneuvers."},{"timestamp": 840, "reason": "Simulation demo", "context": "High-fidelity warehousing simulation showing robot (AGV) and human picker interaction."},{"timestamp": 930, "reason": "Real-world implementation", "context": "Footage of a real-world warehouse facility in Germany implementing trained MARL policies."}]}thumb_upthumb_down
